systems.name: Chord-Melody Paint-by-Numbers Method (Four-Step Recipe)
systems.summary: The book's central thesis and operating system: a four-step chord-melody recipe that drives every later chapter as either a constraint, lookup table, or escape hatch on one of its four steps. Step 1 picks a suitable tune (ballads, not bebop, since slower tempos with longer-held melody notes are practically easier). Step 2 transposes the tune into a workable key where the melody sits on the top 3 strings between roughly the 3rd and 9th frets, avoiding the two failure modes (too high sounds thin, too low sounds dark and muddy). Step 3 learns the melody on the top 2-3 strings, imposing a horizontal rather than positional fretting approach with an explicit carve-out that occasional dips to the 3rd or 4th string are fine. Step 4 voices chords below the melody using the load-bearing painting-by-numbers rule: pick voicings whose top note is the same interval as the melody note relative to the underlying chord, applied to the first melody note of each bar and at chord changes, since the guitar's quick decay lets a chord change imply sustain. The interval-analysis linchpin is governed by the explicit golden rule (intervals relate to the current chord, not the overall key) backed by the major-scale-of-the-current-chord's-root reference and a closed chord-tone vocabulary (1, b9, 9, b3, 3, 11, #11, 5, b13, 13, b7, 7, plus PT). Dictionary lookup is keyed on melody string + chord type + interval, and a dictionary shape is transposed by sliding it until its top note lands on the melody fret.

members.name: Step 1: Pick a Suitable Tune
members.summary: Ballad-not-bebop selection rule: slower tempos with longer-held melody notes are practically easier for beginners.

members.name: Step 2: Transpose Into a Workable Key
members.summary: Find a key where the melody sits on the top 3 strings between roughly the 3rd and 9th frets.

members.name: Step 3: Learn the Melody on the Top 2-3 Strings
members.summary: Horizontal rather than positional fretting on the top strings, with carve-out dips to the 3rd or 4th string allowed.

members.name: Step 4: Voice Chords Below the Melody
members.summary: Painting-by-numbers voicing selection: top note of the voicing equals the melody note's interval relative to the underlying chord.

members.name: Closed Chord-Tone Interval Vocabulary
members.summary: Fixed dictionary index keys: 1, b9, 9, b3, 3, 11, #11, 5, b13, 13, b7, 7, plus PT (passing tone) as escape hatch.

traversal_rules.name: Top-3-Strings, 3rd-to-9th-Fret Range Rule
traversal_rules.summary: Transpose into a key where the melody sits on the top 3 strings between roughly the 3rd and 9th frets to avoid thin (too high) or dark/muddy (too low) results.

references.quote_excerpt: melody sits on the top 3 strings between roughly the 3rd and 9th frets
too high sounds thin, too low sounds dark and muddy

traversal_rules.name: Horizontal Melody Fingering on Top Strings
traversal_rules.summary: The top-string canvas forces a horizontal rather than positional fretting approach, with occasional dips to the 3rd or 4th string permitted.

references.quote_excerpt: horizontal rather than positional fretting approach
occasional dips to the 3rd or 4th string are fine

traversal_rules.name: Transpose Dictionary Shape by Sliding to Melody Fret
traversal_rules.summary: A dictionary voicing is transposed into the tune's key by sliding the shape along the neck until its top note lands on the target melody fret.

references.quote_excerpt: sliding the shape until its top note lands on the melody fret

modification_rules.name: Painting-by-Numbers Voicing Selection Rule
modification_rules.summary: Pick voicings whose top note is the same interval as the melody note relative to the underlying chord; the voicing may be slightly adapted as long as the melody note ends up on top.

references.quote_excerpt: voicings whose top note is the same interval as the melody note
voicing can be slightly adapted as long as the melody note ends up on top

modification_rules.name: Golden Rule: Intervals Relate to Current Chord, Not Key
modification_rules.summary: The most common beginner mistake is labeling intervals against the overall key rather than the current chord; the reference is always the major scale of the current chord's root.

references.quote_excerpt: labeling intervals against the overall key rather than the current chord
reference is always the major scale of the current chord's root

modification_rules.name: Don't Harmonize Every Melody Note
modification_rules.summary: Place chords selectively on long-held notes and at chord changes, exploiting the guitar's quick decay so that a chord change implies sustain rather than holding the note out.

references.quote_excerpt: you don't need a chord on every melody note
placing chords on long-held notes
chord change to imply sustain rather than holding the note out

modification_rules.name: PT (Passing-Tone) Label as Escape Hatch
modification_rules.summary: Unharmonized chromatic melody notes get the PT label rather than a chord-tone interval, and a melody note that anticipates the next chord is labeled against that next chord.

references.quote_excerpt: PT (passing tone) escape hatch for unharmonized chromatic notes
melody note anticipating the next chord is labeled against that next chord

modification_rules.name: Three Remediation Strategies for Awkward Passages
modification_rules.summary: When a strict painting-by-numbers output is unplayable, cut notes out of the chord, substitute parallel octaves or diads, or remove the difficult voicing entirely (the author's most-used escape valve).

references.quote_excerpt: cut notes out of the chord
substitute a different harmonization device such as parallel octaves or diads
remove the difficult voicing entirely

modification_rules.name: Playability Over Ambition (Less Is More)
modification_rules.summary: Easier voicings flow musically better; chords placed selectively are easier on the hands and usually imply the harmony adequately.

references.quote_excerpt: chords placed selectively are easier on the hands
playability beats ambition because easier voicings flow musically better

modification_rules.name: Sanctioned Rhythmic Adjustment of Melody for Fingering
modification_rules.summary: Rhythmic adjustment of the melody is acceptable to facilitate fretboard jumps, framed as a sanctioned rule rather than a compromise.

references.quote_excerpt: rhythmic adjustment of the melody is acceptable to facilitate fretboard jumps

systems.name: Three Harmonic Families Taxonomy
systems.summary: Chapter 2's organizing taxonomy partitions the chord universe into three harmonic families - Major, Minor, and Dominant - with minor refined into m7 and m7b5 sub-families and dominant refined into natural ('7') and altered ('7alt') sub-families. The sub-family distinction is what drives major-vs-minor ii-V-i voicing choice. The chapter also formalizes the chord-construction principles that back the taxonomy: a chord symbol is an attempt to describe a sound, chords are built by stacking thirds, and 9/11/13 extensions are explained as continuing to stack thirds past the 7th. A final terminological rule - surrounding chords define a chord's name as much as its notes do - foreshadows the substitution machinery of Chapter 7.

traversal_rules.name: Sub-Family Distinction Drives ii-V-i Voicing Choice
traversal_rules.summary: Refinement of minor into m7 vs m7b5 and dominant into natural vs altered is what drives major-vs-minor ii-V-i voicing selection.

references.quote_excerpt: sub-family distinction drives major-vs-minor ii-V-i voicing choice

modification_rules.name: Stacking Thirds as Construction Principle
modification_rules.summary: Chords are built by stacking thirds, and 9/11/13 extensions are explained as continuing to stack thirds past the 7th.

references.quote_excerpt: stacking thirds as the core construction principle
9/11/13 extensions as continuing to stack thirds past the 7th

modification_rules.name: Surrounding Chords Define a Chord's Name
modification_rules.summary: Surrounding chords define a chord's name as much as its notes do, which foreshadows substitution machinery.

references.quote_excerpt: surrounding chords define a chord's name as much as its notes do

modification_rules.name: Color Localized in Dominant Extensions and Tensions
modification_rules.summary: The 'jazzy' sound is located specifically in extensions and tensions added to dominant chords as the targeted chord type for color.

references.quote_excerpt: extensions and tensions added to dominant chords as the targeted chord type for color

systems.name: Chord Collection Library (ii-V-I Dictionary Keyed by String Set and Melody Note)
systems.summary: The central indexing unit of the method: a chord collection is a group of chords covering every diatonic interval as the top note, which is what makes the dictionary indexable by melody note. The dictionary is structured around ii-V-I because that single progression covers all three harmonic families, and collections are duplicated into 1st-string and 2nd-string variants to match the top-2-string melody constraint. Chapter 8 consolidates the collections into a four-way split by quality and string set: Major ii-V-I on string set 4321 and on string set 5432, and Minor ii-V-i on the same two string sets with b5 and b9 alterations characteristic of the minor cadence. A separate section catalogues altered/extended dominants as the method's color resource, and a solo-guitar voicing section backs Module 4 with 6th-string-root shell voicings plus a catalog of extensions stacked on those shells.

members.name: Minor ii-V-i, String Set 4321 (b5 / b9 alterations)
Minor ii-V-i, String Set 5432 (b5 / b9 alterations)

traversal_rules.name: Three-Input Dictionary Lookup
traversal_rules.summary: Dictionary lookup is keyed on three inputs: the melody string, the chord type at that moment, and the melody note's interval relative to that chord.

references.quote_excerpt: melody string, chord type, and melody note's interval relative to that chord

traversal_rules.name: 1st-String and 2nd-String Top-Note Variants
traversal_rules.summary: Collections are duplicated into 1st-string and 2nd-string variants to match the top-2-string melody constraint from Module 1.

references.quote_excerpt: 1st-string and 2nd-string variants to match the top-2-string melody constraint

traversal_rules.name: String-Set 4321 vs 5432 Split by Quality
traversal_rules.summary: ii-V-I voicings are catalogued in a four-way split: major and minor cadences each on both the 4321 and 5432 string sets.

references.quote_excerpt: major ii V I on string set 4321 and on string set 5432

references.quote_excerpt: minor ii V i on the same two string sets with the b5 and b9 alterations

modification_rules.name: A Collection Covers Every Diatonic Interval as Top Note
modification_rules.summary: A chord collection is defined as a group of chords covering every diatonic interval as the top note, which is what makes the dictionary indexable by melody note.

references.quote_excerpt: group of chords covering every diatonic interval as the top note

modification_rules.name: ii-V-I as the Single Progression Covering All Three Families
modification_rules.summary: The dictionary is structured around ii-V-I precisely because that single progression covers all three harmonic families.

references.topic: Why ii V I for collections
references.quote_excerpt: ii-V-I covers all three harmonic families

modification_rules.name: Shell Voicing Extensions Stacked on 6th-String-Root Shells
modification_rules.summary: The solo-guitar harmonic vocabulary is built on 6th-string-root shells with a catalog of extensions stacked on top of those shells.

references.quote_excerpt: 6th-string-root shell voicings plus a catalog of extensions stacked on top

systems.name: Voicing Construction Rules (Omittable Notes, Shells, Density)
systems.summary: The load-bearing instrument constraint of the method: the gap between the theoretically correct chord and the playable guitar voicing, filled by an explicit omittable-notes list and a shell-voicing primitive. Chapter 2 establishes the omittable-notes priority - 5th, root, 11th, and 9th first, with 3rd and 7th sometimes omittable in complex chords. Chapter 5 makes the shell voicing (root, 3rd, and 7th only) the primitive for solo-guitar arranging, derived from bar chords by dropping the 5th first, with the 6th as a substitute for the 7th for a warmer color and a general rule to avoid doubled notes unless the doubling happens to sound good. Bass-on-5th/6th-strings prominence is the load-bearing difference from trio playing, with bass-too-close-to-upper-harmony as the diagnostic for thin arrangements and a key-selection preference for A, E, or D so open strings can carry bass.

members.name: Shell Voicing (Root, 3rd, 7th)
members.summary: The solo-guitar voicing primitive: root, 3rd, and 7th only, derived from bar chords.

members.name: Omittable-Notes Priority List
members.summary: 5th, root, 11th, 9th first; 3rd and 7th sometimes omittable in complex chords.

members.name: Bass on 5th and 6th Strings
members.summary: Greater prominence of bass on the 5th and 6th strings since there is no bass player to cover that role.

members.name: Open-String Keys (A, E, D)
members.summary: Prefer A, E, or D so open strings can carry bass notes and free the fretting hand.

traversal_rules.name: Bass-Too-Close-to-Upper-Harmony Diagnostic
traversal_rules.summary: The diagnostic for thin-sounding arrangements is bass voiced too close to the upper harmony.

references.quote_excerpt: bass voiced too close to the upper harmony

traversal_rules.name: Key Selection Favors Open-String Bass (A, E, D)
traversal_rules.summary: Prefer A, E, or D so open strings can carry bass notes and free the fretting hand.

references.quote_excerpt: prefer A, E, or D so open strings can carry bass notes

modification_rules.name: Omittable-Notes Priority: 5th, Root, 11th, 9th, Then 3rd/7th
modification_rules.summary: Bridge the gap between the theoretically correct chord and the playable guitar voicing by omitting in priority order: 5th, root, 11th, 9th first; 3rd and 7th sometimes omittable in complex chords.

references.quote_excerpt: 5th, root, 11th, and 9th, with 3rd and 7th sometimes omittable
gap between the theoretically correct chord and the playable guitar voicing

modification_rules.name: Shell Voicing: Drop the 5th First
modification_rules.summary: The shell voicing (root, 3rd, 7th) is derived from bar chords by dropping the 5th first as the rule for which note to cut.

references.quote_excerpt: shell voicing - root, 3rd, and 7th only
dropping the 5th first as the rule for which note to cut

modification_rules.name: 6th Substitutable for 7th for Warmer Color
modification_rules.summary: The 6th is available as a substitute for the 7th for a warmer, more resolved color.

references.quote_excerpt: 6th available as a substitute for the 7th for a warmer, more resolved color

modification_rules.name: Avoid Doubled Notes Unless the Doubling Sounds Good
modification_rules.summary: General refinement rule: avoid doubled notes unless the doubling happens to sound good.

references.quote_excerpt: avoid doubled notes unless the doubling happens to sound good

modification_rules.name: Voicings Cap at Four Notes (Right-Hand Constraint)
modification_rules.summary: Voicings cap at four notes and the pinky is omitted from the picking hand; plucked notes must sound simultaneously to register as a chord.

references.quote_excerpt: voicings cap at four notes and the pinky is omitted
plucked notes must sound simultaneously to register as a chord
